nativeRange.habitat: Small, clear coastal streams and tributaries in the Brazilian state of Ceará; shallow, well-oxygenated upland drainages over sandy or fine-gravel substrate with leaf litter and riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate to protect delicate barbels
leaf litter (dried catappa or oak leaves)
driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dense planting at tank edges for security

diet.notes: Benthic forager accepting a varied diet of sinking dried foods (micro-pellets, catfish wafers, crushed flake), frozen or live small invertebrates (bloodworms, daphnia, micro-worms, Tubifex), and occasionally algae-based wafers. Should not be relied upon to scavenge tank leftovers; target-feed sinking foods after lights out to ensure adequate nutrition. A varied diet maintains condition and supports breeding readiness.

goodWith: dwarf shrimp (neocaridina, caridina) in species-appropriate conditions
avoidWith: boisterous fast-moving fish that out-compete at feeding

summary: Aspidoras rochai is a tiny armored catfish endemic to coastal streams of Ceará, Brazil. A sister-genus to Corydoras within Callichthyidae, it is distinguished by a separate skull structure and typically reaches only ~5 cm. Peaceful and gregarious, it thrives in groups of 6+ in soft, slightly acidic, well-oxygenated water and tolerates the cooler temperatures of its highland stream habitat.
careNotes:
Aspidoras rochai belongs to the genus Aspidoras — the sister-genus to Corydoras within the family Callichthyidae. Despite their near-identical appearance and behavior, Aspidoras are taxonomically distinct: they possess a different cranial bone structure (the frontoparietal fontanel is absent or greatly reduced) and are not assigned C-numbers. The 2024 Dias/Britto Corydoras revision did not affect Aspidoras, which remains its own valid genus.

This species stays small (~3.5–5 cm) and is considerably less common in the hobby than most Corydoras. It originates from the coastal river drainages of Ceará state in north-eastern Brazil — relatively high-altitude streams that run clear, soft, and somewhat cooler than Amazonian lowlands, explaining its preference for the lower end of the tropical temperature range (20–26°C). Avoid keeping with species requiring warm (28°C+) conditions.

Keep in groups of at least 6 — preferably more — as Aspidoras are social and show stress-related behavioral suppression in smaller numbers. Fine sand substrate is essential to protect the delicate sensory barbels used in benthic foraging; coarse gravel erodes these over time. Like all Callichthyidae, A. rochai is a facultative air-breather and will periodically dart to the surface; ensure the tank has a tight-fitting lid with some air gap. Maintain excellent water quality with stable parameters and mature biological filtration — this species should not be introduced to immature tanks. Moderate, indirect flow is preferred; avoid strong direct currents. A dim or shaded setup with leaf litter closely mimics the natural biotope and reduces stress.
breedingNotes: Breeding occurs in a similar fashion to Corydoras and has been achieved in home aquaria, though Aspidoras rochai is infrequently bred compared to popular Corydoras species. Condition a group (ideally 2–3 males per female) on a varied diet of live and frozen foods. Spawning is typically triggered by a significant water change (30–50%) with cooler, softer water — mimicking the onset of the rainy season — ideally timed with a drop in barometric pressure. Eggs are adhesive and scattered on flat surfaces, plant leaves, or glass. Remove eggs to a separate rearing container or protect with a mesh divider, as adults will consume them. Fry hatch in approximately 3–5 days and accept micro-foods (infusoria, micro-worms, finely crushed flake) from first feeding. Fry are delicate and require pristine water quality.

primary.name: FishBase — Aspidoras rochai
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided maximum size (4.4 cm SL males/unsexed), temperature range (21–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (up to 14 dH), native distribution (Brazil, Ceará State coastal rivers), behavior (schooling, facultative air-breathing), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), IUCN status (Data Deficient, 2018), and diagnostic notes (body stripe pattern, preadipose plate variation). No photographs available on FishBase; only family-level illustration shown.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Aspidoras pauciradiatus (genus proxy)
additional.notes: No SeriouslyFish profile exists for A. rochai. A. pauciradiatus profile used as a close congener proxy for husbandry guidance: temp 20–27°C, pH 6.0–7.0, hardness 36–215 ppm, minimum tank base 60×30 cm, fine sand substrate, omnivore diet (sinking foods plus live/frozen invertebrates), groups of 4–6 minimum, Corydoras-style breeding triggered by cool water change. Care parameters and breeding method applied to A. rochai with appropriate adjustments.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Corydoras/cory catfish care guide used as supplementary source for Callichthyidae husbandry norms applicable to Aspidoras: group size (6+), substrate (smooth sand or fine gravel), diet (sinking foods, live/frozen bloodworms, not primarily algae eaters), breeding triggers (cooler water change, conditioning on varied foods), compatibility (peaceful community). Temperature guidance for cooler-tolerant cories (72–82°F / 22–28°C) cross-referenced against FishBase data.

additional.name: FishBase — Aspidoras rochai species page (IUCN/ecology notes)
additional.notes: Secondary pass for ecological notes: benthopelagic habitat classification, facultative air-breathing confirmed, schooling behavior confirmed, IUCN Data Deficient status (assessed November 2018), low fishing vulnerability score. No depth-range data available; estimated 0–1 m based on stream habitat type. No image candidates identified — FishBase shows no photographs for this species and Wikimedia Commons search returned zero results.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeds in captivity using Corydoras-style method: cool water change triggers spawning. Eggs are adhesive and scattered; adults consume eggs if not separated. Fry are delicate and require micro-foods and pristine water. Infrequently bred in the hobby due to limited availability of the species.
